scenario: A Web Modeler project has 8 people working in it. The team wants different permission levels: 2 admins can manage settings, 4 editors can modify resources, 2 viewers can only read. They need to assign roles.
question: How does Web Modeler manage per-project permissions?

options.text: Role-based access at the project level. md:27-32 there are exactly four roles: (1) Project Admin -- edits the project + folders + diagrams + invites collaborators; (2) Editor -- edits folders + diagrams; (3) Commenter -- cannot edit or invite but can view diagrams/properties and leave comments; (4) Viewer -- read-only, no comments. Members are invited by email and assigned exactly one role. Organization-wide elevated access exists separately via super-user mode (:45-49) requiring Organization Owner/Admin or a Web Modeler Admin role.

All members added to a Web Modeler project automatically receive equal permissions with no ability to grant different access levels to different team members -- this description does not reflect the actual behaviour. Web Modeler supports role-based access control at the project level. Project members can be assigned different roles such as Viewer, Editor, and Admin, each with different permission levels. Viewers can read but not modify models. Editors can create and modify resources. Admins can manage membership and project settings. This role-based permission model enables appropriate access control across teams with different responsibilities.

Web Modeler project permissions can only be configured through the Web Modeler REST API with no user interface available for managing member access -- this description does not reflect the actual behaviour. Web Modeler provides a graphical user interface for member and permission management within the project settings. Project admins can invite new members, assign roles, and remove members through the UI without needing API access. The Web Modeler REST API also provides programmatic access to the same membership management capabilities for teams that want to automate onboarding workflows.

SSO integration controls authentication: who can log in to Web Modeler. Web Modeler provides additional authorisation controls beyond authentication: role-based project-level permissions that determine what each authenticated user can do within projects. SSO determines whether a user can access the application; Web Modeler's permission system determines what that user can create, modify, or delete within specifichas its own role authorisation layer.

a.text: Correct. Web Modeler implements role-based access at project granularity per collaboration/collaboration.md:27-32. Each project has its own member list with role assignments. Exactly four roles (do not confuse with C7 or other tooling): Project Admin -- edits the project + all folders/diagrams + invites collaborators. Editor -- edits all folders + diagrams (no member management). Commenter -- cannot edit folders/diagrams or invite users; can view diagrams + properties and leave comments (per :201 -- Admin/Editor/Commenter can add comments). Viewer -- read-only; cannot edit nor comment. Practical workflow: project owner invites a member by email → picks one of the four roles → member accesses per permission level. For org-wide elevated access, super-user mode (:45-49) temporarily grants Project Admin across all projects -- requires Organization Owner/Admin (SaaS) or a Web Modeler Admin role (SM) per :59-67.

d.text: Incorrect. SSO is authentication; RBAC is the authorisation layer on top.

Viewer -- read-only; cannot edit nor comment. Practical workflow: project owner invites a member by email → picks one of the four roles → member accesses per permission level. For org-wide elevated access, super-user mode (:45-49) temporarily grants Project Admin across all projects -- requires Organization Owner/Admin (SaaS) or a Web Modeler Admin role (SM) per :59-67.

findings.message: option text contains internal source/line reference
